questions.prompt: What does a Git commit contain?

choices.text: A complete SNAPSHOT of every file (as a tree), pointers to parent commit(s), and metadata (author, timestamp, message). Snapshots, not deltas — same content is deduplicated via content addressing (identical files = same blob hash). The commit's SHA-1 hash IS its ID, computed from content; commits are immutable. Amending creates a new commit with a new hash.

questions.explanation: Snapshot + parent pointers + metadata. Content-addressed; immutable.

questions.prompt: You realize you've been making commits in detached HEAD state. How to save them?

choices.text: Create a branch BEFORE switching away: `git switch -c new-branch-name`. The branch is created at the current commit; your detached-HEAD work is now on a named branch, safe to push. If you've already switched away without saving, the reflog has them: `git reflog` shows HEAD movements; `git checkout HEAD@{N}` returns to the desired point; then create the branch.

questions.explanation: Create a branch with `git switch -c name`. Or recover via reflog if already switched.

choices.text: Never rebase commits pushed to a SHARED branch. Rebase replaces commits with new hashes; teammates who pulled the originals will have divergent history, force-push creates conflicts and confusion. Safe to rebase: your local feature branch (not yet pushed) or pushed only to your own fork. Unsafe: shared main/develop or any branch teammates have pulled. Rewrite only history you own.

questions.explanation: Never rebase shared commits. Rewriting commits others have pulled creates pain.

choices.text: Delete and regenerate: `rm package-lock.json && npm install`, then `git add && git commit` (or rebase --continue). Lockfiles are auto-generated artifacts encoding precise dep resolution; hand-merging usually produces broken JSON or inconsistent versions. Same pattern for yarn.lock, pnpm-lock.yaml, Cargo.lock, Gemfile.lock — regenerate, don't merge. Standard across ecosystems.

questions.explanation: Delete and regenerate lockfiles. Hand-merging breaks them.

questions.prompt: Way to clean up a PR with 12 messy commits before merging?

choices.text: Use 'Squash and merge' on GitHub — combines all 12 PR commits into one clean commit on main with a meaningful message. Feature branch retains the original commits (deletable post-merge); main gets one tidy commit per feature. Alternative: interactive rebase locally (`git rebase -i HEAD~12`) to reorder/squash/reword commits into 1-3 meaningful ones, then regular merge. Squash-and-merge is the dominant 2026 pattern for teams valuing clean main history; pair with Conventional Commits format for changelog generation.

questions.explanation: Squash and merge (GitHub) or interactive rebase to clean up before merging.
